data.title: Here's How Much You Would Have Made Owning abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF Stock In The Last 5 Years
data.description:
abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF (NYSE:SIVR) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 15.26% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 26.22%. Currently, abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF has a market capitalization of $6.09 billion.

Buying $100 In SIVR: If an investor had bought $100 of SIVR stock 5 years ago, it would be worth $317.09 today based on a price of $79.50 for SIVR at the time of writing.

abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF's Performance Over Last 5 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

data.title: 2 "Safety Plays" to Make Now
data.description:
Right now, a lot of investors are trying to pick “the perfect stock” in the middle of one of the most chaotic market environments we’ve seen in years.

And frankly, that’s the wrong game to be playing.

When the markets are calm, sure, you can hunt for the company with the best earnings report, strongest balance sheet, or hottest product launch.

But when the world is in complete turmoil? Individual stocks become wildly unpredictable.

And right now, we’re dealing with a storm that’s being fueled by forces much bigger than any one company.

So, instead of trying to find the needle, why not trade the whole haystack?

Here’s what I mean—and two safety plays you make…

Focus on Sector ETFs, Not Individual Companies

An exchange-traded fund (ETF) gives you exposure to an entire industry instead of betting the farm on one company.

So if gold is rising, you don’t have to guess which mining company will outperform, you trade a gold ETF. 

If regional banks bounce, you don’t have to guess which bank will lead, you can trade the ETF. 

If oil continues to surge, as it looks like it will until the Strait of Hormuz reopens—you simply trade the sector itself. 

And that approach comes with several advantages:

Reduced Gap Risk

Individual stocks can collapse overnight on bad earnings. ETFs rarely move that violently because they hold dozens of companies.

Simplified Analysis

You only need to be right about the direction of the sector—not one company.

Better Options Liquidity

Major ETFs, like the two I’m about to show you, have extremely deep options markets. That gives traders much more liquidity and freedom to move their funds in and out. 

And that’s what you want when facing an unusual combination of global and domestic shocks all at once.

First, we’re watching a major geopolitical escalation unfold in the Middle East following the launch of Operation Epic Fury. We’re now in day 13 of the war, with no real end in sight—or clear idea of what victory would look like. 

This isn’t just another headline that disappears next week.

Energy markets, global shipping lanes, and the critical Strait of Hormuz are suddenly under pressure.

When something this big happens on the geopolitical stage, it sends shockwaves across the global financial system.

That’s exactly why I’m turning my attention to ETFs. And there are two that I like the most right now when it comes to safety—and profits. 

The first one is a bullish move…

Safety Trade #1: Gold

When uncertainty explodes, investors historically run toward one asset: gold.

And as of the time of writing, gold prices are pushing toward record highs—recently crossing $5,100 per ounce.

And since mining profits are leveraged to gold prices, GDX often amplifies gold’s move.

The second is a bearish play…

Safety Trade #2: Regional Banks

Although global tensions are dominating headlines (and rightfully so), the domestic economy still matters. And regional banks are the lifeblood of U.S. small business lending.

Right now, the market is beginning to speculate that the Federal Reserve may eventually pivot to support the weakening labor market. That means regional banks could see a technical bounce.

But the seasonal data reveals a different truth about SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (NYSE:KRE)—it historically falls between now and mid-April:

So doing a bearish trade on KRE may be the move here, regardless of what happens in the Middle East.
